Description: Generic phishing email asks users to verify their account via a link.
Users click unknown links without checking the destination.
Finance or admin staff receive fake invoice emails from bogus suppliers.
A phishing email directs staff to a fake but obviously poor login page.
Staff spot suspicious emails but are unsure how to report them.
Callers pretend to be IT support asking for temporary passwords.
User opens an attachment that attempts to drop basic malware.
Users choose simple or reused passwords for internal systems.
Passwords are written on sticky notes near desks or screens.
Teams share single logins for shared mailboxes or apps.
Accounts for staff who left months ago are still enabled.
Accounts do not lock after many failed login attempts.
Cloud admin portals only require a password with no extra checks.
Remote VPN access relies only on passwords and is widely used.
Teams store information without any sensitivity labels.
Staff keep small datasets of personal data on unencrypted laptops.
Cloud sharing links are sometimes created with 'anyone with the link' access.
The same spreadsheets exist on multiple unmanaged devices.
Staff use standard USB sticks to move data between home and office.
Shared network folders grant 'everyone' read access by default.

ScenarioName: Backups Stored on Shared Drive Only
Description: Backups are stored as simple copies on a shared drive without protection.
Non-critical servers expose RDP/SSH to the internal network without justification.
Printers and minor network devices use factory default credentials.
Guest Wi-Fi uses a simple password shared widely over time.
User PCs and servers share the same network segment.
Some endpoints run OS versions that have not been patched for several months.
Smart TVs or cameras are connected without security review.
Internet edge router runs out-of-support firmware with known issues.
